All reports
NEXQLOUD SEALED

Compliance Guide for Review

A short alert layer: what we noticed in your market and your interviews that you ought to confirm before you publish.

Prepared by Fenn and Hart
June 2026

0. How to Use This Guide, and Our Role

Read this first. We are your marketing and strategy partner, and this guide is not legal advice and not a compliance audit. It is a short list of things we noticed in your market and heard in your own interviews that you ought to be aware of and confirm with a qualified professional, an attorney or a regulatory specialist, before you publish. We flag and frame; we never clear a claim as compliant. The decision and the legal sign-off belong to you and your counsel, who are the right people to confirm anything here.

This guide is keyed to the claims your strategy actually recommends in the Strategic Alignment Brief and the Marketing & Messaging Guide, and it covers your marketing communication only. It does not cover employment, tax, corporate, securities, or product-engineering questions; if one of those comes up, take it to counsel.

A note on jurisdiction. We have written to US federal rules as the default, because your primary market is the United States and most of what follows is federal. Your strategy also names the United Kingdom and European Union as adjacencies, and several US states have their own advertising and privacy laws. Treat any state-level and international rule below as a question for counsel, not a settled answer.

1. What We Noticed (the alerts)

A handful of considerations stand out for Sealed’s marketing. We have kept this to the few that carry real weight for your strategy and left the full regulatory survey to counsel.

The baseline that governs everything you say

  1. The alert. The FTC expects every marketing claim, stated or implied, to be truthful and substantiated before it runs, and it expects you to hold a reasonable basis for any objective claim at the moment you make it.1 For an AI capability claim specifically, the FTC has said the support must be real technical evidence, and a marketer should not exaggerate what an AI product can do.2
  2. Why it matters for your marketing. Your whole position rests on objective, checkable claims: “verifiable,” “tamper-evident,” “hardware-signed,” “a regulator can confirm what happened.” Each of those is the kind of claim the FTC treats as needing evidence in hand before you publish it.
  3. Confirm with a professional. Ask counsel to confirm you hold competent, current evidence for each capability claim you run, and that the evidence exists before the claim goes live, not after.

Your three core claims are still awaiting production proof

  1. The alert. Leadership told us Sealed is at design-partner stage with no production cohort yet, and flagged three claims as awaiting production evidence: turnkey deployment without a rebuild, a regulator-accepted audit trail, and governed closed-model use. The FTC has been active here. It announced Operation AI Comply in September 2024, a sweep against AI claims companies could not substantiate, with the message that there is no AI exemption from the law.3 The SEC settled its first “AI washing” cases in March 2024 against two advisers for AI claims they could not document.4
  2. Why it matters for your marketing. A claim that a regulator accepts the audit trail, or that deployment is turnkey, reads as a present-tense outcome. At design-partner stage you may not yet hold production evidence for it.
  3. Confirm with a professional. Ask counsel how to frame the three design-partner-stage claims so they describe what is filed, live, and in design-partner pilots without implying a production result you cannot yet support. The MMG already states the production gap plainly in its objection-handling and differentiator sections.

Absolute security language is a known enforcement target

  1. The alert. The FTC treats unqualified security and encryption claims as deceptive when the security does not match the words, and it has acted on this with Zoom, Henry Schein, and Illuminate Education.5 Leadership set its own red line on this in the interviews: claim governance and blast-radius containment with verifiable proof, and never claim breach prevention.
  2. Why it matters for your marketing. Words like “breach-proof,” “unbreakable,” “100% secure,” or an unqualified “cannot be altered” sit squarely in the high-risk band. They are also indefensible for a containment-and-proof product.
  3. Confirm with a professional. Confirm that every security line uses the containment-with-proof framing your own founder set and carries evidence for any specific security representation. The MMG’s banned-word glossary and the “We Do Not Say” table already encode this.

Comparative claims name a rival and carry the same evidence burden

  1. The alert. Comparative claims that name or categorize a competitor are allowed, and they are held to the same substantiation standard as any other claim.6 Where the comparison is about an AI product’s superiority, the FTC expects competent comparative support.2
  2. Why it matters for your marketing. Your strategy leans on comparisons: “guardrails produce no hardware-attested receipt,” “a contract states what is allowed and Sealed proves what happened,” “confidential-compute vendors cannot enclave a closed third-party model.” Each names or categorizes a competitor and invites a check on whether the comparison is accurate and supported.
  3. Confirm with a professional. Ask counsel to confirm you hold documented support for each comparative assertion, specific to the named or categorized competitor, before it runs.

Your statistics and credentials need to say exactly what is true

  1. The alert. Several claims carry their own rules. A SOC 2 is an attestation report, so the AICPA position is that you may say you have a SOC 2 report, while the word “certified” stays off it, and the SOC logo carries its own usage terms.7 CMMC Level 2 is verified by an independent assessor, so it is a status a company holds only once that assessment is passed, and misrepresenting CMMC or NIST 800-171 status in connection with federal work carries False Claims Act exposure under the DOJ Civil Cyber-Fraud Initiative.8 With five patents filed and none yet granted, the accurate term is “patent pending,” and using “patented” before a patent issues can be false marking under federal law.9 Leadership also set an internal rule that every third-party statistic (IBM, PwC, Deloitte) must carry its source.
  2. Why it matters for your marketing. These are the claims most likely to drift in copy: “certified,” “patented,” “CMMC compliant,” or a statistic with no citation. Each drift turns a true position into a risky one. The parent company’s patents and SOC 2 belong to the parent, so presenting them as Sealed’s own is its own accuracy problem.
  3. Confirm with a professional. Confirm the exact wording for SOC 2 (“report available under NDA”), CMMC (“Level 2 targeted September 2026,” forward-looking), and patents (“patent pending” or “patents filed”), and confirm which credentials belong to NexQloud Sealed versus the parent. The MMG already carries each of these in defensible form.

Design-partner references need a disclosed connection

  1. The alert. Under the FTC Endorsement Guides, any connection between you and an endorser that could affect how much weight a reader gives the endorsement, and that a reader would not expect, has to be disclosed clearly, and a testimonial should not imply typical results you cannot support.10
  2. Why it matters for your marketing. Your strategy plans to use design-partner references and quotes. A design-partner relationship is exactly the kind of connection a reader would not assume and the Guides expect you to disclose, and at pre-production stage a design-partner quote should not imply a typical production outcome.
  3. Confirm with a professional. Ask counsel how to disclose the design-partner relationship on any reference or quote, and how to qualify results so they do not read as typical production performance.

2. Claim Flags

The specific claims your strategy makes that are worth a check before you publish. We name where each appears and point to the defensible version your MMG already carries. We do not clear any of these; we flag them for confirmation.

The claim What to confirm The defensible version (already in your MMG)
“Sealed is the only AI-governance layer that hands a regulated organization a hardware-signed, independently verifiable receipt” (source-of-truth line, MMG Section 1; hero options, Section 9.1) That you hold evidence for “hardware-signed” and “independently verifiable,” and that the “only” superiority claim is supported across the named field [confirm with a professional]1 Lead on the differentiation fact rivals structurally lack, and describe Sealed’s own receipt as filed, live, and at design-partner stage with production proof still ahead (MMG Pillar 1, Section 7.1).
“A regulator can confirm what happened without ever contacting the company” (headline claim, MMG Pillar 1) That this is framed as what the receipt is designed to let a regulator do, not as a regulator-accepted outcome you have already achieved [confirm with a professional]3 State the production gap plainly; hold “regulator accepted the audit trail” until a production reference clears it (MMG Section 7.2 objection-handling; Appendix A).
“Govern the AI. Prove every action” and “blast-radius containment with verifiable proof” (taglines and four-gate copy, MMG Sections 5.2, 6.2) That the containment-and-proof framing is used everywhere and that no surface drifts to “breach-proof,” “unbreakable,” or “100% secure” [confirm with a professional]5 Containment with verifiable proof, never breach prevention; the banned-word glossary maps each absolute to its defensible replacement (MMG Section 10).
Comparative lines: “guardrails produce no hardware-attested receipt,” “a contract states what is allowed and Sealed proves what happened,” “confidential-compute vendors cannot enclave a closed third-party model” (MMG Sections 7.1, 7.2, 8.2) That you hold documented support specific to each named or categorized competitor for each comparison [confirm with a professional]6 Keep the comparison anchored to the sourced competitive facts and the structural gap each rival has (MMG Section 7).
“SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” and any use of the SOC logo (credential copy, MMG Section 8.2) That you never say “SOC 2 certified,” and that any logo use follows AICPA terms [confirm with a professional]7 “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA,” and the report cited as the parent’s where it is the parent’s (MMG Sections 8.2, 7.2).
“CMMC Level 2 targeted September 2026” (why-now and credential copy, MMG Pillar 5) That CMMC is always stated as a forward-looking target, never as a held certification, in any federal-facing material [confirm with a professional]8 “Targeted September 2026,” framed as forward-looking, mapped to the defense vertical (MMG Pillar 5, Section 8.2).
“Five patents filed” / “patent pending” (credential copy, MMG Section 8.2; objection-handling, Section 7.2) That copy never says “patented” while the five applications are filed and pending [confirm with a professional]9 “Five patents filed on the architecture,” “patent pending,” with parent DCP patents named separately from Sealed-specific filings (MMG Sections 8.2, 7.2).
Statistics: “67% of executives say generative AI grew their attack surface” (PwC); “only 21% report a mature governance model” (Deloitte) (MMG Pillar 3, Section 3.0) That each statistic carries its named source wherever it appears, per leadership’s own rule [confirm with a professional]2 Each figure stated with its source named (MMG Pillar 3; the founder’s claims-discipline rule in MMG Section 0).
Design-partner reference or quote, once one can be named (MMG Pillar 4, Section 7.2) That the design-partner relationship is disclosed, and the result is not implied as typical production performance [confirm with a professional]10 “A single attributable design-partner account, once one can be named,” with the relationship disclosed and the stage stated (MMG Pillar 4).

3. A Quick Channel Check

Only the channels your strategy puts in play. One line each, naming the one thing to confirm.

  • Web and homepage. Every objective claim on a Sealed-owned surface is the FTC’s truth-and-substantiation case in its most public form; confirm each hero and proof line holds evidence before it goes live.1
  • Email outreach. Commercial email is governed by the CAN-SPAM Act, which expects accurate headers and subject lines, a valid physical postal address, and a working opt-out you honor within ten business days; confirm your sending setup and opt-out flow meet it.11
  • Paid ads. Paid placements carry the same FTC truth-and-substantiation standard as any other claim, plus each ad platform’s own policies; confirm both the claim and the platform rules before you run a campaign.1
  • Testimonials and design-partner references. The FTC Endorsement Guides expect a clear disclosure of any material connection and no implication of typical results you cannot support; confirm the disclosure and the qualifier on every reference.10
  • Lead lists and prospect data. California’s CCPA and CPRA now cover B2B contact data, so a prospect’s work email and title are personal information with notice and opt-out obligations, and UK or EU prospects bring GDPR into play; confirm how your lists are built and processed with counsel.12

4. What to Take to Your Professional

A short checklist, framed as questions, to confirm before you publish.

  • Do we hold competent, current evidence for each objective capability claim (“verifiable,” “tamper-evident,” “hardware-signed,” “a regulator can confirm what happened”) before it runs?1
  • How should we frame the three design-partner-stage claims (turnkey-without-rebuild, regulator-accepted audit trail, governed closed-model use) so they do not imply a production result we cannot yet support?3
  • Is every security line in containment-with-proof language, with no absolute security claim anywhere on any surface?5
  • Do we hold documented support specific to each named or categorized competitor for every comparative claim?6
  • Are SOC 2 (“report available under NDA,” never “certified”), CMMC (“Level 2 targeted September 2026,” forward-looking), and patents (“patent pending,” never “patented”) worded correctly, and are parent-company credentials clearly separated from Sealed’s own?7 8 9
  • Does every third-party statistic carry its named source, and is every design-partner reference disclosed and properly qualified?2 10
  • Does our email setup meet CAN-SPAM, and have we confirmed how our lead lists are collected and processed under CCPA/CPRA?11 12
  • Jurisdiction: we have assumed US federal rules as the default. Which state advertising and privacy laws apply to us, and what UK and EU rules apply to our UK and EU outreach? State “little FTC Act” and UDAP statutes can run in parallel with the federal rules and vary by state.13

5. What We Looked At

Sources

  • FTC Policy Statement Regarding Advertising Substantiation, ftc.gov, 1984 (currently published); FTC Act Section 5, 15 U.S.C. Sec. 45.
  • FTC Business Blog, “Keep your AI claims in check,” ftc.gov, Feb. 27, 2023.
  • FTC press release, “FTC Announces Crackdown on Deceptive AI Claims and Schemes” (Operation AI Comply), ftc.gov, Sept. 25, 2024.
  • SEC press release and settled orders, In re Delphia (USA) Inc. and In re Global Predictions Inc., sec.gov, March 18, 2024.
  • FTC privacy and security enforcement actions under Section 5 (In re Zoom Video Communications, 2020-2021; In re Henry Schein Practice Solutions; In re Illuminate Education), ftc.gov.
  • FTC Statement of Policy Regarding Comparative Advertising, 16 CFR 14.15, ftc.gov; FTC, “Keep your AI claims in check,” Feb. 27, 2023.
  • AICPA SOC for Service Organizations Logo Guidelines and SOC trademark and usage terms, aicpa-cima.com.
  • DoD CMMC Program final rule, 32 CFR Part 170 (effective Dec. 16, 2024); DOJ Civil Cyber-Fraud Initiative / False Claims Act enforcement, justice.gov, 2024-2025.
  • 35 U.S.C. Sec. 292 (false marking), law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/292 (as amended by the America Invents Act, 2011).
  • FTC Guides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials in Advertising, 16 CFR Part 255 (revised rule effective July 26, 2023), ecfr.gov.
  • CAN-SPAM Act, 15 U.S.C. Sec. 7701 et seq.; FTC, “CAN-SPAM Act: A Compliance Guide for Business,” ftc.gov (penalty update Jan. 2024).
  • CCPA/CPRA, Cal. Civ. Code Sec. 1798.100 et seq. (B2B exemption expired Jan. 1, 2023), CA Attorney General, oag.ca.gov/privacy/ccpa; EU/UK GDPR (state and international specifics flagged for counsel).
  • State UDAP / “little FTC Act” statutes vary by state and carry no single federal citation [confirm with counsel].
